items.q: Which energy system fuels a marathon?
items.why: Students associate hard exercise with anaerobic systems. Long-duration steady-state work uses AEROBIC metabolism (oxygen + carbs/fats) - efficient but slower to ramp up. Anaerobic systems dominate brief intense efforts.

items.q: What is the rough formula for maximum heart rate?
items.why: Students misremember the formula. 220 - age is the classic estimate (rough; varies individually). A 20-year-old's estimated max HR is around 200 bpm; an 80-year-old's is around 140.

items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.
The answer examiners want is: Maximum rate of O₂ consumption. “Maximum heart rate” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The Fick equation expresses VO₂ as:
items.why: It's Q × (a-v) O₂ difference. “HR × respiratory rate” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If Q = 25 L min⁻¹ and (a-v)O₂ difference = 0.16 L L⁻¹, VO₂ (L min⁻¹) is?
items.why: Exact answer: 4.0. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
